summary: Charity Majors shares the advice Honeycomb gave employees during the pandemic: acknowledge that what's being asked is literally impossible, shed everything except your top priority, and spend zero energy on performing normalcy. The core message is that 30 minutes of focused creative energy on the biggest problem beats five hours of distracted availability theater.
key_points: What everyone is being asked to do during the pandemic is literally impossible — and that's okay to acknowledge
Success comes not from doing everything but from saying no to the right things
Performative busyness wastes precious energy while doing nothing to advance real goals
Optimize for focused creative time on the biggest problem, not for appearing available
Having bad days is expected — teams exist to carry each other through the hardest spots
money_quotes: We succeed as a company not by doing everything on our list, but by saying no to the right things; by NOT-doing enough most things so we can focus on the few things we have identified that matter most.

I would rather have 30 minutes of your creative energy applied to our biggest problem today than five hours of your distracted split-brain, juggling, trying to keep up with chat and seem like you're as available per usual today.

Please spend zero energy on performing like you're doing work, or acting extra-responsive, or keeping up a front like things are normal and you're doing fine. That performance costs you precious energy, while doing nothing to get us closer to our goals.
If you're not a parent, remember that you too are allowed to feel overwhelmed and underwater. Just because some have it even harder, doesn't invalidate what you're going through.
key_insight: In crisis periods, leaders should explicitly give teams permission to shed work and stop performing normalcy, redirecting all available energy toward the single highest-impact problem.
